# Writing Trust — an agent's guide

<!-- @generated by `cargo xtask gen-docs` from the trust-lints rule registry.
     Do NOT edit by hand — change `crates/trust-lints/src/rules.rs` and
     regenerate. CI enforces freshness via `cargo xtask gen-docs --check`. -->

Trust is a strict dialect of Rust for LLM-authored code. Write code that obeys
the rules below and `trust check` passes; break one and it's a compile-time
error carrying the same `instead:` fix you see here. Load this file into your
context before writing Trust and you'll comply on the first try instead of
iterating against the checker.

**Two grammar extensions** (lowered to plain Rust before `rustc`):
- **Named arguments** past arity 1: call `f(width: 1920, height: 1080)`, not
  `f(1920, 1080)`. `trust fix` inserts the names for you.
- **A pipe operator** for chaining free functions onto a receiver — see
  `docs/SPEC.md` for the grammar.

**Activation:** `[package.metadata.trust] strict = true` in `Cargo.toml` for
whole crates built with `cargo trust build`; `#![strict]` at the top of a file
for single-file `trust check` or per-file opt-in. See `docs/SPEC.md` for the
full grammar and per-rule detail.

## Rules

### R0001 · no-unwrap

- **Why:** panics on None/Err are silent control flow; agents reach for `.unwrap()` reflexively
- **Instead:** propagate with `?`, or `.expect("why this can't fail")`

### R0002 · empty-expect

- **Why:** empty messages defeat the point of `.expect()`
- **Instead:** give `.expect("…")` a real message explaining why it can't fail

### R0003 · no-as-cast

- **Why:** `as` silently truncates and is a frequent source of integer-overflow bugs
- **Instead:** use `T::try_from(x)?` for fallible casts, or `.into()` for widening

### R0004 · no-glob-import

- **Why:** glob imports hide which symbols are in scope and let unrelated changes affect resolution
- **Instead:** import the specific items: `use foo::{A, B};`

### R0005 · justify-unsafe

- **Why:** every `unsafe` block must explain the invariant being upheld
- **Instead:** precede the `unsafe` block with a `// safety: …` comment

### R0006 · justify-allow

- **Why:** every `#[allow]` must explain why the rule is being suppressed
- **Instead:** precede the `#[allow(…)]` with a `// reason: …` comment

### R0007 · no-impl-trait-return

- **Why:** anonymous return types kill local reasoning; name the type with an alias
- **Instead:** name the type with a `type Alias = …;` and return the alias

### R0008 · no-user-macros

- **Why:** macros expand non-locally; agents misuse them frequently
- **Instead:** inline the logic, or opt in with `#[strict::macros_ok]`

### R0010 · no-todo-macro

- **Why:** `todo!()` / `unimplemented!()` ship as runtime panics; finish or fence behind `cfg(test)`
- **Instead:** finish the implementation, or return a typed `Err`

### R0011 · no-panic

- **Why:** explicit panics drop typed errors on the floor; return `Err` and let the caller decide
- **Instead:** return a typed `Err` and let the caller decide whether to abort

### R0012 · no-bool-param

- **Why:** raw `bool` parameters are positional footguns; named enums make intent self-documenting
- **Instead:** replace the `bool` with a named enum, e.g. `enum Mode { On, Off }`

### R0014 · no-bare-index

- **Why:** `v[i]` panics on out-of-bounds; `.get(i)` makes the failure path explicit
- **Instead:** use `.get(i)` and handle the `Option`

### R0017 · no-same-type-params

- **Why:** adjacent same-type parameters are silently swappable; named args fix the call site but not values built into the wrong variable — distinct newtypes make a swap a type error
- **Instead:** wrap each in a distinct newtype — `trust_std::newtype!(pub Width(u32));`

### R0015 · allow-missing-reason

- **Why:** every `#[allow(trust::Rxxxx)]` must include a `reason = "..."` justification
- **Instead:** add a `reason = "…"` argument to the `#[allow(trust::…)]`

### R0016 · allow-unknown-code

- **Why:** `#[allow(trust::Rxxxx)]` references a rule code that is not in the registry
- **Instead:** use a real rule code (run `trust explain` for the catalogue)

### R0042 · no-positional-args

- **Why:** positional argument ordering is the largest LLM-authored bug class in Rust; named args eliminate it
- **Instead:** name the arguments — `f(width: …, height: …)` — or run `trust fix`
